text: Dysregulation of alternative splicing in prostate cancer is linked to transcriptional programs activated by AR, ERG, FOXA1, and MYC. Here, we show that FOXA1 functions as the primary orchestrator of alternative splicing dysregulation across 500 primary and metastatic prostate cancer transcriptomes. We demonstrate that FOXA1 binds to the regulatory regions of splicing-related genes, including HNRNPK and SRSF1 . By controlling trans- acting factor expression, FOXA1 exploits an “exon definition” mechanism calibrating alternative splicing toward dominant isoform production. This regulation especially impacts splicing factors themselves and leads to a reduction of nonsense-mediated decay (NMD)-targeted isoforms. Inclusion of the NMD-determinant FLNA exon 30 by FOXA1-controlled oncogene SRSF1 promotes cell growth in vitro and predicts disease recurrence. Overall, we report a role for FOXA1 in rewiring the alternative splicing landscape in prostate cancer through a cascade of events from chromatin access, to splicing factor regulation, and, finally, to alternative splicing of exons influencing patient survival.
summary: FOXA1 functions as the primary orchestrator of alternative splicing dysregulation across 500 primary and metastatic prostate cancer transcriptomes. FOXA1 binds to the regulatory regions of splicing-related genes including HNRNPK and SRSF1. By controlling trans-acting factor expression, FOXA1 exploits an "exon definition" mechanism that calibrates alternative splicing toward dominant isoform production. This regulation especially impacts splicing factors and reduces nonsense-mediated decay–targeted isoforms. Inclusion of the NMD-determinant FLNA exon 30 by FOXA1-controlled oncogene SRSF1 promotes cell growth in vitro and predicts disease recurrence. FOXA1 thus rewires splicing from chromatin access to splicing-factor regulation to exon changes that influence patient survival.

text: Del Giudice et al. identify the pioneer transcription factor FOXA1 as a master regulator of alternative splicing in prostate cancer. By controlling splicing factors, FOXA1 buffers the noise of isoform production toward a mRNA dominant product. This regulation impacts on splicing of nonsense-mediated decay-determinant exons influencing patient survival.
summary: In prostate cancer, FOXA1 is identified as a pioneer transcription factor and a master regulator of alternative splicing. It controls splicing factors and buffers the noise of isoform production toward a dominant mRNA product. This regulation impacts splicing of nonsense-mediated decay-determinant exons and influences patient survival.
